description:
You are a security auditor who must verify that a small, locally-hosted API correctly enforces access
    controls.  Your goal is to launch a lightweight HTTP test service, probe a handful of URLs with
    curl, and record the results in a **strictly formatted log file** that our automated grader will
    read.

    1. Create the working directory  
       /home/user/api_audit  

    2. Inside that directory write a Python 3 script called  
       /home/user/api_audit/restricted_server.py  
       that starts an HTTP server on port 9000 and behaves as follows:
       • GET /public            → status 200 and a JSON body: {"status": "public"}  
       • GET /admin             → status 403 and a JSON body: {"error": "forbidden"}  
       • Any other path (for example /missing) → the normal http.server 404 response.  
       Implement this by subclassing http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler; you do not need root
       privileges, and the script must listen on 127.0.0.1 only.

    3. Start the server in the background (do not block your terminal; you may use & or a subshell).
       Wait briefly so it is ready to accept connections.

    4. Use curl to perform **exactly five** permission-checking requests in the order listed
       below.  You may supply any options you like as long as the request method and endpoint do not
       change and no response body is written to the terminal.  
       a.  GET  http://127.0.0.1:9000/public  
       b.  GET  http://127.0.0.1:9000/admin  
       c.  GET  http://127.0.0.1:9000/missing  
       d.  HEAD http://127.0.0.1:9000/public  
       e.  HEAD http://127.0.0.1:9000/admin  

       Hint: the option combination “-s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}'” is often used to extract just
       the status code.

    5. For every request, append one line to the log file  
       /home/user/api_audit/permissions_report.log  
       immediately after the request returns.  **The log file must contain exactly six lines, each
       terminated by a single newline (no blank lines at the end).**

       • Lines 1–5 record the test results in this mandatory fixed-width format:  
         <METHOD> <PATH> <STATUS_CODE>  
         – METHOD is the literal text GET or HEAD (uppercase).  
         – PATH  is exactly /public, /admin, or /missing.  
         – STATUS_CODE is the three-digit HTTP response code returned by curl.  
         Separate the three fields with a single space; do not include the host, scheme, or port.

       • Line 6 is a summary line with this exact format:  
         SUMMARY total_tested=5 pass_count=5

       An example of the **precise** expected contents is shown below (your delivered file must match it
       character-for-character, including case and spacing):

       GET /public 200
       GET /admin 403
       GET /missing 404
       HEAD /public 200
       HEAD /admin 403
       SUMMARY total_tested=5 pass_count=5

    6. When you are done, you should have:
       • /home/user/api_audit/restricted_server.py  (the server implementation)  
       • /home/user/api_audit/permissions_report.log (the six-line report described above)  
       • The server process still running in the background is fine but not required for grading.

    The automated tests will:
       1. Read /home/user/api_audit/permissions_report.log and verify it matches the six-line example
          exactly.  
       2. Confirm that /home/user/api_audit/restricted_server.py exists and is a valid Python file.

    No other output is required; console chatter is allowed during the procedure but will not be
    checked.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth data used by the grader.  **Do not reveal in the public task description.** -->
    <file path="/home/user/api_audit/permissions_report.log"><![CDATA[
GET /public 200
GET /admin 403
GET /missing 404
HEAD /public 200
HEAD /admin 403
SUMMARY total_tested=5 pass_count=5
]]></file>
    <file path="/home/user/api_audit/restricted_server.py">
        <!-- The grader only checks the file exists and is readable; content not strictly enforced. -->
    </file>
